html:
Inkeverse Group, LSX:A2JQPN / 3700.HK

Inkeverse is not the “Soul hidden inside a live-streaming shell” story from older drafts. The filings do not support that. The real thesis is cleaner and stranger: a discarded Chinese live-social platform is trading below net cash and far below liquid financial assets while quietly shifting revenue toward short-form drama/content distribution, overseas social products, AI-assisted entertainment workflows, and Web3/crypto exposure.

At about EUR0.088 per German share, the equity value is roughly USD185-205 million depending on venue and FX. Against that, the company ended 2025 with RMB2.12 billion of cash, RMB270 million of term deposits, RMB974 million of FVPL financial assets, and RMB414 million of crypto assets, versus only RMB397 million of financial liabilities and RMB736 million of total liabilities. Owner equity was RMB4.61 billion, or roughly EUR0.30 per share. The stock is therefore around 0.3x book and below net cash before giving value to the operating business, content library, investment portfolio, or crypto reserve. Source: 2025 annual report, current German quote.

The market is not completely irrational. 2025 revenue fell 25.2%, MAUs fell 10.8%, ARPU fell 4.4%, and net operating cash flow was weak at RMB51 million. Also, reported operating profit was helped by RMB343 million of net other gains, mostly fair value gains on financial assets. This is not a pristine compounder.

But the variant perception is that the stock is not priced as “messy but solvent and optional.” It is priced as if the cash pile will be incinerated and the operating business is worth less than zero. That is too harsh.

The operating transition matters. Value-added services, mainly Inke, Duiyuan, and other live/social products, fell from RMB5.04 billion to RMB3.66 billion. But entertainment content service rose from RMB1.31 billion to RMB1.37 billion, and revenue sharing from content distribution partners jumped from RMB593 million to RMB1.07 billion. Inkeverse is moving from pure live-streaming monetization toward content distribution, mini dramas, and overseas social entertainment. Management explicitly says 2026 focus is AI-enabled social entertainment, AIGC content production, and overseas expansion across Southeast Asia, MENA, and other high-potential regions.

That transition has torque because the current valuation gives it no credit. If the legacy live-social base merely stabilizes and content distribution continues scaling, a business doing RMB5 billion-plus of revenue does not need heroic margins to justify the whole market cap. A normalized RMB200-300 million owner-earnings business plus liquid assets gets to a share price well above today. If the overseas mini-drama/social product strategy works, the stock can re-rate from “melting China live-streaming app” to “asset-backed, profitable, AI-content/social option.”

Capital allocation is the main risk and the main catalyst. The old draft overstated buybacks. 2025 buybacks were only RMB6.8 million, and the board declared no dividend for 2025. Instead, management is allocating cash into crypto, AI, and fintech. Some of that is rational option buying, some of it deserves a discount. The company agreed to invest RMB100 million in Skywork AI, a connected-party Kunlun AI/AIGC asset, though later news said the deposit was returned while approvals remained pending. It also agreed to subscribe HK$300 million of GoFintech Quantum shares at HK$1.78 per share. Those moves explain why the market refuses to pay full value for the balance sheet.

Still, even aggressive haircuts leave value. If cash, term deposits, FVPL assets, and crypto are haircut 35%, net of financial liabilities, the liquid stack is still around RMB2.0-2.2 billion before the operating business. Current market value is roughly RMB1.4-1.6 billion. The bear case requires continued revenue erosion plus bad capital allocation. The base case only needs asset value leakage to slow and content/social earnings to normalize.

Social and retail context is useful because there is almost no crowd here. The German line is thin and should be treated as a convenience quote, not the real liquidity pool. The Hong Kong line is the relevant market. English retail discussion is sparse; automated quote pages mostly frame the stock as volatile, shrinking, and low quality. That absence of sponsorship is part of the setup. Inkeverse has exposure to the exact themes investors chase elsewhere, AI content, short dramas, overseas entertainment, Web3, but because it sits inside a small Hong Kong-listed Chinese live-streaming name with messy accounting, nobody pays for it.

The most important 2026 confirmations are not revenue growth alone. The right checklist is: value-added services stop falling at a 2025 pace, content distribution keeps growing, operating cash conversion improves after the 2025 working-capital cleanup, no large related-party deal destroys the cash discount, and management either resumes meaningful buybacks or initiates a dividend. Any one of those can move the stock. Two or three together can force a re-rating.

Targets, using EUR0.088 current price:

Bear: EUR0.055, downside 37%. Core social revenue keeps shrinking, content growth stalls, and management keeps reallocating liquid assets into low-confidence investments.

Base: EUR0.22, upside 150%. The stock trades to roughly 60% of haircut liquid value plus a modest value for normalized earnings. This is still below book value.

Bull: EUR0.45, upside 411%. Content/overseas growth works, AI tooling lowers production costs, and management returns capital or makes the asset discount impossible to ignore. This is not a blue-sky software multiple, just a move toward full asset value plus 8-10x a revived earnings base.
